s: The stall that beat Gordon Ramsay at his own chicken rice
w: Started 1987 as a two-person stall at Maxwell, run by a brother and sister. In a televised 2013 showdown its chicken rice beat Gordon Ramsay outright; Bourdain reportedly said the rice alone was worth eating plain. Michelin Bib Gourmand since 2016 - the queue never got shorter.
_facts: Opened 1987 by Mdm Foo Kui Lian and her brother at Maxwell Food Centre
Won Michelin Bib Gourmand in the inaugural Singapore guide, 2016
Beat Gordon Ramsay's chicken rice in a 2013 televised Hawker Heroes challenge
Anthony Bourdain praised the rice as good enough to eat on its own

n: Hill Street Tai Hwa Pork Noodle
s: Singapore's only Michelin-starred hawker stall, bak chor mee division
w: Tang Joon Teo started ladling out black-vinegar bak chor mee from a Hill Street cart in the 1930s; the recipe passed to his son, then three grandsons. It drifted to Marina Square and then a Kallang coffee shop, picking up a Michelin star in 2016 - the queue went from thirty minutes to two hours overnight.
_facts: Founded 1930s on Hill Street by Tang Joon Teo
Son Tang Chay Seng took over when father fell ill in the 1960s; three grandsons inherited it after his 1995 death
Relocated Hill Street to Marina Square in the 1990s, then to Crawford Lane, Kallang in 2004
One of the first two street-food stalls in the world awarded a Michelin star, 2016; queue times jumped from 30 minutes to 2 hours

n: Hawker Chan (Liao Fan Hong Kong Soya Sauce Chicken Rice & Noodle)
s: The 2-dollar plate that made Michelin-star history
w: Chan Hon Meng left Hong Kong for Singapore at fifteen and apprenticed under a Hong Kong chef before working out his own soya-sauce chicken. He set up at Chinatown Complex in 2009 at two dollars a plate; in 2016 it won a Michelin star, one of the first two street stalls anywhere to do so. Lost it in 2021.
_facts: Founder Chan Hon Meng left Hong Kong for Singapore at age 15, apprenticed under a Hong Kong chef
Started Liao Fan Soya Sauce Chicken Rice & Noodle at Chinatown Complex Food Centre in 2009
Won Michelin star in 2016, one of the first two street-food stalls in the world to do so, at S$2 a dish
Lost the Michelin star in 2021; brand now trades internationally as Hawker Chan

s: Charcoal-fired laksa, eaten with a spoon only since 1956
w: Wong Yew Hwa got the recipe from a Hainanese friend and started pushing a laksa cart along Sungei Road in 1956, splitting hours between Johor Road by day and here by night at twenty cents a bowl. The gravy still simmers over charcoal for that smoked edge - a spoon only, no chopsticks, same as always.
_facts: Founded 1956 by Wong Yew Hwa, recipe from a Hainanese friend named Ah Tong
Originally a pushcart, sold on Johor Road by day and Sungei Road by night at 20 cents a bowl
Broth still heated over charcoal for a smoked flavour
Served only with a spoon, no chopsticks, in distinctive chicken-motif bowls

s: The diamond-shaped egg tart that became Singapore's own
w: Fong Chee Heng pushed a drinks cart round Pasir Panjang in the late 1920s before opening a shop on Smith Street in 1935, adding Cantonese pastries as it grew. Land acquisition moved the family to South Bridge Road in the 1970s, where a fourth generation bakes the diamond egg tart the whole city is judged against.
_facts: Founder Fong Chee Heng started as a streetside drinks stall in Pasir Panjang in the late 1920s
Opened a shop at 33 Smith Street in 1935, expanding into Cantonese pastries
Relocated to South Bridge Road in the late 1970s after government land acquisition of Smith Street
Diamond-shaped egg tart became the iconic mould in the 1980s; now run by a fourth-generation owner

s: Murtabak opposite Sultan Mosque, unchanged since 1908
w: Haji Mahmud, a Keralan-Indian immigrant, opened his restaurant on North Bridge Road in 1908, and it's stayed put across from Sultan Mosque ever since. The murtabak goes on an extra base of plain prata before egg and minced meat pile on - a house quirk outlasting every trend, marked at its centenary with a giveaway.
_facts: Founded 1908 by Haji Mahmud, a Keralan-Indian immigrant
Located on North Bridge Road opposite Sultan Mosque
Murtabak served on an extra base layer of plain prata before egg and minced meat
Marked its centenary with a public giveaway on 20 March 2008

s: The coffee stall that turned kaya toast into a national dish
w: Loi Ah Koon arrived from Hainan in 1926, spending a decade working other men's coffee stalls before opening his own at Telok Ayer Basin in 1936, formally registered as Ya Kun in 1944. His wife's homemade kaya, spread thick on charcoal toast, built a coffee stand into an institution, family-run for fifty years.
_facts: Founder Loi Ah Koon emigrated from Hainan to Singapore in 1926
Set up a coffee stall at Telok Ayer Basin in 1936; formally registered as Ya Kun in 1944
Wife's homemade kaya (egg and coconut spread) became the signature
Remained a small family stall for decades before rapid franchised expansion from 1999

s: Singapore's oldest surviving Hainanese coffeeshop, since 1919
w: Hainanese immigrants opened this coffeeshop on Killiney Road in 1919 as Kheng Hoe Heng, outlasting most of its contemporaries. Regular Woon Tek Seng bought the place in 1992 and renamed it for its street. The 67 Killiney Road original is the oldest existing Hainanese coffeeshop in the Singapore Book of Records 2008.
_facts: Founded 1919 on Killiney Road by Hainanese immigrants, originally named Kheng Hoe Heng Coffeeshop
Bought by regular customer Woon Tek Seng, also Hainanese, in 1992, who renamed it Killiney Kopitiam
The 67 Killiney Road branch listed in the Singapore Book of Records 2008 as the Oldest Existing Hainanese Coffeeshop
Fresh kaya made daily in-house

s: A Glasgow-cast Victorian octagon that became a satay street
w: The current structure went up in 1894 on reclaimed land, its ironwork cast at Walter MacFarlane's Glasgow foundry to keep the old market's octagonal plan and radial aisles for airflow. MRT works in the 1980s meant it was dismantled and rebuilt over the tunnels beneath. Renamed Lau Pa Sat in 1989 for its satay street.
_facts: Current building completed 1894 on reclaimed land, replacing the original 1823 Telok Ayer Market
Cast-iron structure prefabricated by Walter MacFarlane & Company of Glasgow, retains octagonal plan with eight radial aisles
Dismantled and rebuilt piece by piece in the 1980s to allow MRT tunnel construction beneath it
Renamed Lau Pa Sat in 1989 as a festival market; adjoining Boon Tat Street closes for open-air satay stalls at night

s: The unlicensed hawker cluster that got tamed into a legend
w: Serangoon Gardens Estate was British-planted in the 1950s; by the late 1960s unlicensed hawkers crowding its traffic circus caused enough friction the council built them a home. It opened in 1972 as Serangoon Gardens Hawkers' Centre, a name a committee changed to Chomp Chomp. Sambal stingray off the grill draws crowds.
_facts: Serangoon Gardens Estate developed in the early 1950s by engineer Steven Charles Macey for British RAF personnel, streets named after Britain
Built in 1972 at 20 Kensington Park Road to resettle unlicensed street hawkers from Serangoon Garden Circus
Originally named Serangoon Gardens Hawkers' Centre; renamed Chomp Chomp by a hawker committee, from the Teochew term Tiong Tiong
Famed for charcoal-grilled BBQ sambal stingray and satay

s: A hawker centre built on the runway of a defunct airport
w: Kallang Airport opened in 1937 as Singapore's first civilian airfield and closed in 1955; when the estate was redeveloped for housing, the food centre that followed in 1972 was built on top of the old runway. It opened with 176 stalls, the most modern hawker centre of its time. Over 150 stalls trade there still.
_facts: Kallang Airport, Singapore's first civilian airport, opened 1937 and closed 1955
Old Airport Road Food Centre built 1972 as part of the Kallang Airport Estate redevelopment, on the site of the old runway
Opened as an emporium-cum-hawker-centre with 176 food stalls on the ground floor, costing S$2.5 million
Home to over 150 hawker stalls today

s: A market that outgrew the buffalo pens it is named for
w: This corner of Little India was called Kandang Kerbau - Malay for buffalo pens - for slaughterhouses that worked here until the 1920s; Hokkien traders knew it as Tek Kia Kha, foot of the small bamboos, for groves along Rochor Canal. The market dates to 1915, rebuilt in 1982, reverting to Tekka by 2000.
_facts: Roots trace to 1915; area originally called Kandang Kerbau (buffalo pens) for slaughterhouses operating until the 1920s
Hokkien name Tek Kia Kha ('foot of the small bamboos') gave rise to Tekka
Relocated to current concrete structure in 1982, operating as Zhujiao Centre
Renamed back to Tekka Centre in 2000; renovated in 2008

s: The laksa that beat Gordon Ramsay across the street from where it began
w: Nancy Lim worked the laksa stall at 49 East Coast Road for years before rent hikes pushed the owner out, then her too - so in 1998 she opened her own place across the street at number 51. The noodles come cut short, eaten with a spoon only, the Katong way. A televised win over Gordon Ramsay in 2013 made it a landmark.
_facts: Founder Nancy Lim (Nancy Koh) previously worked at a laksa stall at 49 East Coast Road
Opened her own stall at 51 East Coast Road in 1998 after rent increases
Served with only a spoon, no chopsticks or fork, noodles cut short
Beat Gordon Ramsay in a 2013 cook-off; won Michelin Bib Gourmand in 2017

s: 260 stalls that rehoused Chinatown's roving street hawkers in one go
w: Three hundred itinerant hawkers working Chinatown's roadsides for decades were moved in when this complex opened on 1 October 1983, first named Kreta Ayer Complex before the current name stuck a year on. Singapore's largest hawker centre, over 260 stalls, where a chicken rice stall won a Michelin star in 2016.
_facts: Opened 1 October 1983, built to resettle roadside itinerant hawkers from surrounding Chinatown streets
Cost S$18 million; 300 street hawkers moved in at opening
Originally named Kreta Ayer Complex, renamed Chinatown Complex in 1984
Singapore's largest hawker centre with over 260 stalls; former home of Michelin-starred Liao Fan Hong Kong Soya Sauce Chicken Rice & Noodle

s: A wet market and hawker centre atop Singapore's oldest housing estate
w: Tiong Bahru was Singapore's first public housing estate, built through the 1930s in the Art Deco curves that still ring the market at its centre. The current building - wet market below, hawker stalls above - replaced the original in 2006, but the chwee kueh and lor mee stalls trace their regulars back generations.
_facts: Tiong Bahru is Singapore's first public housing estate, built from the 1930s in Art Deco style
Current market building (wet market ground floor, hawker centre above) opened in 2006, replacing the original market on the same site
Long-running stalls for chwee kueh and lor mee are among its best known
Sits at the heart of a conservation estate managed by the Singapore Improvement Trust and later HDB
